p-2.plaintext: Add data attributes to your rendered HTML to enable progressively richer visual editing:

children.text: Add data attributes to your rendered HTML to enable progressively richer visual editing:

ul-3.plaintext: data-block-uid="blockId" — Click-to-select blocks. Hydra.js adds click handlers and shows a blue outline and Quanta toolbar on selected blocks. data-edit-text="fieldName" — Inline text editing. For simple text, click and type directly. For rich text (slate widget), select text to apply formatting via the Quanta toolbar. data-edit-media="fieldName" — Visual media uploading. Editors can upload, pick or drag-and-drop images directly onto the element. data-edit-link="fieldName" — Link editing. Click behaviour is replaced with a link picker to select content, enter an external URL, or open the link.

children.text: — Click-to-select blocks. Hydra.js adds click handlers and shows a blue outline and Quanta toolbar on selected blocks.
— Inline text editing. For simple text, click and type directly. For rich text (slate widget), select text to apply formatting via the Quanta toolbar.
— Visual media uploading. Editors can upload, pick or drag-and-drop images directly onto the element.
— Link editing. Click behaviour is replaced with a link picker to select content, enter an external URL, or open the link.

ul-9.plaintext: Attributes without selectors apply to the root element: block-uid=xxx Attributes with selectors target child elements: edit-text=title(.card-title) Closing <!-- /hydra --> marks end of scope Self-closing <!-- hydra block-uid=xxx /--> applies only to next sibling element

p-15.plaintext: Every `data-edit-*` attribute — `data-edit-text`, `data-edit-link`, `data-edit-media` — takes a Unix-style **field path**, resolved the same way for all three. A path has two independent axes:

children.text: , resolved the same way for all three. A path has two independent axes:

ul-17.plaintext: fieldName — this block's own field (default) ../fieldName — the parent block's field ../../fieldName — the grandparent block's field /fieldName — a page/root field

p-18.plaintext: `..` always steps up one **block** — never an object or region level (see below).

children.text: — never an object or region level (see below).

p-30.plaintext: `data-block-readonly` is *your* call — use it when your frontend wants to lock a block for its own reasons (a teaser mirroring another page, a listing item).

children.text: call — use it when your frontend wants to lock a block for its own reasons (a teaser mirroring another page, a listing item).

p-31.plaintext: You do **not** need it for template content. Hydra already knows which blocks a template marks read-only from the block data and enforces that itself, so your renderer doesn't need to detect template blocks or mark them.

children.text: need it for template content. Hydra already knows which blocks a template marks read-only from the block data and enforces that itself, so your renderer doesn't need to detect template blocks or mark them.

p-33.plaintext: When rendering Slate nodes to DOM, your renderer must follow these rules for `data-node-id`:

children.text: When rendering Slate nodes to DOM, your renderer must follow these rules for

ol-34.plaintext: Element nodes (p, strong, em, etc.) must have data-node-id matching the Slate node's nodeId Wrapper elements — If you add extra wrapper elements around a Slate node, ALL wrappers must have the same data-node-id as the inner element

children.text: Wrapper elements — If you add extra wrapper elements around a Slate node, ALL wrappers must have the same

p-35.plaintext: hydra.js uses node-ids to map between Slate's data model and your DOM. When restoring cursor position after formatting changes, it walks your DOM counting Slate children.

children.text: hydra.js uses node-ids to map between Slate's data model and your DOM. When restoring cursor position after formatting changes, it walks your DOM counting Slate children.

tabs.label: Html
tabs.code: Valid wrapper pattern: bold Both elements have the same node-id, so they count as one Slate child. Invalid (missing node-id on wrapper): bold This breaks cursor positioning because hydra.js can't correlate DOM structure to Slate structure.

h-37.plaintext: Non-editable content inside a slate field

children.text: Non-editable content inside a slate field

p-38.plaintext: Sometimes a renderer adds elements to slate output that are **not** part of the editable content — a decorative icon (an "opens in a new tab" glyph), a generated chip, an embedded non-editable widget. These have no `data-node-id` (they aren't Slate nodes), and they must be marked so that **both** the editor's caret and hydra's DOM→Slate reader skip them:

p-43.plaintext: hydra's DOM→Slate reader skips any child (without a `data-node-id`) that carries **either** attribute — treating it as chrome, not content. Without this, the element's text would be read back into the Slate value on every edit / select / delete over it, corrupting the value.

h-46.plaintext: One top-level node per slate field

children.text: One top-level node per slate field

p-47.plaintext: A slate field's `value` is an array, but it always holds exactly **one top-level node** — a single paragraph, heading, list, or blockquote. Inline content (bold, links, …) lives in that node's `children`.

p-48.plaintext: Editing can transiently produce more than one top-level node — pasting multiple paragraphs, pressing Enter, or a Backspace that demotes a list item to a paragraph (`[ul, p]`). Hydra normalizes that immediately:
